response:
## What `src/billing.ts` does

It's a 310-line billing module containing 5 top-level functions. The two named ones are:
- `generateInvoice` (lines 50–253) — a 204-line "god function" mixing unrelated responsibilities
- `calculateRefundPreview` (lines 40–44) — name suggests pure calculation, but body calls `saveRefundAudit` and `sendRefundEmail` (side effects)

The module deals with the `"enterprise"` plan concept and encodes a refund rule in a comment near line 39: *"Only owners can refund enterprise plans unless billing support approves."* It reads the wall clock directly in many places (7× `Date.now()` / `new Date()` across lines 57–273).

## Related files I'd want to read before editing

- **`src/billing.test.ts`** — its only two tests are both flagged as weak (one has 0 asserts, the other uses only weak matchers). So existing tests won't catch regressions.
- **`src/api/plan.ts`, `src/jobs/plan-sync.ts`, `src/ui/pricing.ts`** — all three duplicate the `"enterprise"` literal that billing uses. Any policy change here likely needs to ripple to those.
- **`src/date.ts`** — billing's `Date.now()`/`new Date()` calls probably belong behind helpers here; this file has its own pile of temporal crimes (mixed UTC/local, unsafe parse, DST-naive math) that could leak into billing's behaviour.

## Risks the scan flagged on `src/billing.ts`

| Severity | Charge | Where |
|---|---|---|
| high | God Function (`generateInvoice`, 204 lines) | L50–253 |
| medium | Temporal Recklessness (7× direct `Date`) | L57, 63, 92, 198, 238, 250, 273 |
| medium | String Sprinkles (`"enterprise"` in 4 files) | L7 + 3 sibling files |
| medium | False Identity (`calculateRefundPreview` does I/O) | L40–44 |
| medium | God File (310 lines, threshold 300) | whole file |
| medium | Logic in the Alibi (refund rule lives in a comment) | L39 |
| low | Unfinished Business (4 TODO/FIXME markers) | L183–308 |

Editing risk concentrates around `generateInvoice` (size hides cross-section coupling), the refund-comment rule (easy to miss when changing refund logic), and the `"enterprise"` literal (any policy edit here needs to be mirrored in three siblings, or centralised first).
